subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear definition of the key concept (âchangeâ) and consistent references throughout the analysis
Strong, company-specific connections to Starbucks in multiple sections
weaknesses: Early introduction lacks explicit roadmap for weaving âchangeâ through each analytical paragraph
Some STEEPLE and pricing analysis paragraphs do not explicitly tie back to the concept of change
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a strong understanding of âchangeâ as the key concept and repeatedly links it to Starbucksâ boycott, CSR initiatives and strategic adaptations. However, the integration is not fully seamlessâoccasional analytical sections proceed without explicitly invoking the conceptâso it aligns best with the 4-mark descriptor rather than the top band.

strengths: Includes exactly four reputable supporting documents from diverse outlets
Documents are generally relevant to Starbucksâ boycott narrative
weaknesses: Lacks annotations explaining how each document informs the analysis or ties into the concept of change
Evidence is used mainly for headline claims rather than deep exploration or contrasting data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student provides the required number of sources and they are on-topic, the depth of engagement is superficial. Without annotations or richer quantitative detail, the supporting materials offer only basic backing, meriting a mid-band allocation of 2 marks.

strengths: Appropriate selection of STEEPLE, dynamic pricing and stakeholder analysis frameworks
Methodology paragraph clearly states chosen tools
weaknesses: Application often drifts into narrative description with no quantified weighting or clear causal linkage
Several STEEPLE factors (e.g., environmental, legal) are omitted, leaving analysis unbalanced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Tools and theories are adequately identified, but their use lacks rigor and completeness. The absence of key factors in STEEPLE and limited contextualization justify a low-band score of 1 mark for selection and application.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Use of company statements and CSR figures to contextualize social threats
Inclusion of customer quotes and share-price graph enriches the stakeholder perspective
weaknesses: Data use is sporadic and superficial (few quantitative metrics, no elasticity calculations)
Several analyses are incomplete or rely on quotations without deeper evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student moves beyond pure description by offering some analytical commentary, but the evaluation lacks depth and consistency. Sporadic data use and incomplete factor discussions place this work in the lower middle band, meriting 2 marks.

strengths: Conclusion explicitly answers the research question and remains consistent with evidence
Synthesizes main findings in a logically coherent manner
weaknesses: Offers no new insights or strategic recommendations
Lengthy restatement of prior analysis rather than concise synthesis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the conclusion fully addresses the question and aligns with the analysis, it lacks depth and actionable recommendations. This performance corresponds to 2 out of 3 marks.

strengths: Clear table of contents and logical progression from introduction through conclusion
Research question is prominently stated and focused
weaknesses: Some page numbers are missing in the contents, reducing navigability
Occasional paragraph breaks are needed for clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The IA has an appropriate overall structure, but minor omissions in navigation and paragraphing limit its effectiveness. This warrants 1 out of 2 marks.

strengths: All required IA elements (title, research question, word count, bibliography, supporting docs, figures) are present
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formats and split URLs reduce readability
Graph captions lack axis labels and clear descriptions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although all formal presentation elements are included, multiple formatting issues and citation inconsistencies hinder the reportâs professionalism. This aligns with 1 out of 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows IB guidelines: title page, table of contents, logical section headings and coherent paragraphing.  The sequence flows from introduction through analysis to conclusion without major disorganisation, matching the âappropriate structure throughoutâ descriptor (2 marks).

The documents (Bloomberg/IBD share-price article, BBC feature, Vox commentary, Fortune opinion) are all germane to Starbucksâ boycott narrative and offer differing viewpoints, but the student mines them only for headline claims rather than deep quantitative detail or contrasting statistics.  Hence they are relevant and of sufficient depth for adequate support, yet do not provide the rich, wide ârange of ideasâ expected for the 4-mark band.
All required HL IA elementsâtitle, research question, word count, bibliography, supporting documents, appendices/figureâare present.  Therefore the top presentation band is earned.

Exactly four supporting documents are provided, meeting the 3-5 requirement.  This aligns with bands 2â4; quality differentiation is handled in the relevance strand, so a middle allocation of 3 marks is appropriate here.

Each chosen tool is applied to the boycott context; STEEPLE sections classify threats/opportunities and the dynamic-pricing discussion links price hikes to consumer backlash.  Nonetheless, the analysis sometimes drifts into narrative description, and the causal link between tools and the research question is not always explicit (e.g., no quantified STEEPLE weighting, limited pricing elasticity data).  This fits the descriptor: ârelevance not always clear but adequately applied.â

The student incorporates limited but serviceable data: a year-to-date share-price graph, a 1.8 % global sales decline, a 3 % U.S. comparable-store dip.  These figures are appropriate but not extensive, and several claims rely on quotations rather than data.  Such usage is âadequateâ rather than âsufficientâ or âeffective,â matching the 3-mark band.

The analysis moves beyond description by weighing alternative explanations (boycotts vs. weak growth expectations) and linking STEEPLE findings to brand impact.  However, evaluation remains surface-levelâno financial modelling or stakeholder-mapping depthâand integration of data is sporadic.  This represents âsome analysisâ with partial integration, suiting the 3-mark descriptor.
The student directly re-states and answers the research question, summarising the degree of impact.  Given that explicit alignment is present, this meets the highest band for addressing the question.

The IA employs STEEPLE, dynamic pricing and basic stakeholder analysis, covering both external-environment and marketing tools.  While these choices are appropriate, the selection is not especially extensive and omits other potentially revealing frameworks (e.g., brand-equity models, Ansoff).  This corresponds to an âadequate selectionâ rather than a limited or highly effective suite.

The concluding section synthesises the earlier discussion, explicitly answers the research question and remains in line with the presented evidence (boycotts affect the brand âto an extentâ while acknowledging other factors).  Though concise, it is logically consistent, fulfilling the top band for this criterion.

Throughout the IA the discussion remains firmly centred on Starbucks: background context, boycott chronology, pricing decisions, stock-price trends and CSR initiatives are all company-specific.  Analysis repeatedly links these issues to brand image and stakeholder reactions, demonstrating solid integration.  A few general statements about Gen Z or social media sit outside the firm, so the link is not absolutely seamless across every paragraph, aligning best with the âstrong analysis with partial integrationâ level.

The student clearly defines the key concept of change and revisits it in several analytical sections (e.g., shifting consumer behaviour, need for CSR adaptations, requests for strategic adjustments).  References to change, however, tend to appear as brief sign-posts rather than being deeply woven into every analytical paragraph; large parts of the STEEPLE and pricing sections analyse impact without explicitly tying back to the transformation theme.  Overall, this shows a strong understanding with partial but not ubiquitous integration, best matched to the 4-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and lists all major sections, making navigation straightforward.
Use of direct company statements to illustrate social threats is effective in contextualizing the analysis.
Environmental and legal factors are not addressed in your STEEPLE, leaving the analysis unbalanced.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Include environmental and legal perspectives in your STEEPLE to provide a more comprehensive threat/opportunity evaluation.
CSR discussion effectively ties sustainability initiatives to brand trust, showing good data use.
The dynamic pricing section cites percentage changes but does not explain how price elasticity was assessed.
Consider incorporating an elasticity calculation or referencing academic data on price sensitivity to deepen the pricing analysis.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and focused, providing a solid foundation for the investigation.
Labelling âChangeâ as the key concept is good, but early elaboration on how this concept will be woven throughout the analysis is missing.
Several subsection entries lack corresponding page numbers, which reduces the usability of the contents listing.
The opening paragraph is overly long and contains run-on sentences; consider breaking it into two or more sentences for clarity.
In the background section, explicitly connect historical facts about Starbucks to the concept of change that underpins your analysis.
How might the foundersâ early innovations signal Starbucksâ capacity for strategic change amid boycotts?
The methodology paragraph clearly states the use of STEEPLE and dynamic pricing, aligning chosen frameworks with the research question.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The methodology description lacks specifics on how data will be collected, measured or validated, reducing transparency of your approach.
Consider detailing the process for weighting or scoring STEEPLE factors to move beyond a purely descriptive application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Definition of STEEPLE is accurate and concise, demonstrating correct selection of this analytical tool.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The political threat discussion ends mid-analysis, suggesting an incomplete exploration of the factorâs impact.
Explicitly link each STEEPLE finding back to âchangeâ to strengthen conceptual integration throughout the analysis.
Standardize the citation format and ensure each URL is complete and clickable to enhance presentation quality.
Supporting documents list is varied and includes a range of reputable outlets, strengthening your evidence base.
The supporting document entries lack annotations explaining their specific relevance to your analysis.
Add brief annotations to each supporting document to clarify how it informs your analysis and ties into âchange.â
Inclusion of customer quotes and Figure 1 enriches the stakeholder perspective analysis.
The stockâprice graphâs caption lacks axis labels and a clear description of the timeframe and magnitude of changes.
Label both axes and describe key inflection points in the text to improve clarity of data interpretation.
The paragraph under âBoycotts Are Not the Reasonâ¦â ends abruptly mid-statement, indicating an editing oversight.
Ensure all paragraphs are complete and explicitly balance external viewpoints with your own evaluative insights.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes analysis and explicitly revisits the research question, showing consistency with evidence.
The conclusion restates prior content at length without offering new insights or strategic recommendations.
Condense the conclusion to highlight key strategic implications and actionable recommendations for Starbucks.
Bibliography includes diverse sources, demonstrating thorough research with textbooks and reputable websites.
Several URLs are split across lines or lack proper formatting, which may render them non-functional.
